concepts.title: Summation and Difference of suffixes of binomial coefficients
concepts.conceptBody:
Summation and Difference of suffixes of binomial coefficients

(a) If the difference of the lower suffixes of binomial cofficient in each term is same.

For eg: ${{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}+{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}+\ldots $. etc.

Then

Case-1: If each term is positive, then

${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{C}}_{0}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+$……… ${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$……. (i)

Interchanging ' 1 ' and ' $\text{x}$ ' :

${{(\text{x}+1)}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}+$…….. ${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}$ …… (ii)

Then multiplying (i) and (ii), and equate the coefficient to suitable power of ' $\text{x}$ ' on both sides.

Case-II: If terms are alternately positive and negative

Then:

${{(1-\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}$ $~={{\text{C}}_{0}}-{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}\ldots \ldots \ldots $(1)

And ${{(\text{x}+1)}^{\text{n}}} ={{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-1}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots .+{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}$…………… (2)

The multiplying (1) and (2), and equate the coeft

Note : $[($ Odd - number $)/2]=8$

(2) If the sum of the lower suffixes of binomial - coefficients in each term is same.

For Ex:

${{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-1}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-2}}+\ldots \cdots \cdots +\cdots +{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}$

Then:

Case-I: If each term is positive, then

${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{C}}_{0}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{x}2-\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots +{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$………… (1)

and ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+$…….$+{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$…………. (2)

Then multiplying (i) and (ii), and equate the coefficient of suitable power of ' $\text{x}$ ' on both sides.

Case-II : If terms are alternately positive and negative,

The ${{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{C}}_{0}}+{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}-\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$………… (1)

and \((1-x)^n=C_0-C_1 x+C_2 x^2+ \ldots \ldots \ldots .+(-1)^n C_n x^n \ldots \ldots (2)\).

Then multiplying (i) and (ii), and equating the coefficient of suitable power of ' $\text{x}$ ' on both sides.

Example

Show that the middle term in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{2n}}$ is $1.3.5\ldots ..\left( 2n-1 \right)/\left( n! \right).{{2}^{n}}{{x}^{n}}$, ' n ' being a positive integer. 

Solution: 

The no. of terms in ${{(1+\text{x})}^{2\text{n}}}$

$=2\text{n}+1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (odd)}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

It's middle term $=\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)/2$

$=\left( n+1 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ term}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_{\text{n}+1}}={{2}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$

$=2n!/\left( n!xn! \right)\cdot {{x}^{n}}$

$=2\text{n}\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)$…….. 4.3.2.1/( $n!xn!)\cdot {{x}^{n}}$

$=[\{\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{n}-3 \right)$3.1.} $\{2\text{n}\left( 2\text{n}-2 \right)$ $4.2\}]/\left( n!xn! \right)\cdot {{x}^{n}}$

$=[\{1.3.5$ $\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\}2\text{n}\{1.2$ $n\}]/\left( n!xn! \right)\cdot {{x}^{n}}$

$=[\{1.3.5$ $\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\}\cdot 2\text{n}]/\left( \text{n}!\text{x}n! \right)\cdot {{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$

$={{\text{T}}_{\text{n}+1}}=1.3.5-\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)/\left( \text{n}! \right)\cdot {{2}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}$

Example

Find the term independent of ' x ' in the expansion of 

(i) $\left( 1+x+2x3 \right){{\left[ \left( 3{{x}^{2}}/2 \right)-\left( 1/3x \right) \right]}^{9}}$ 

(ii)$\left[ \left( {{x}^{1/3}} \right. \right.$ ${{\left. +{{x}^{-1/5}} \right]}^{8}}$ 

Solution: 

(i) $\left( 1+\text{x}+2{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)\left\{ {{\left[ \left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}-\left( 1/3\text{x} \right) \right]}^{9}} \right.$

$=\left( 1+\text{x}+2{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)\left\{ {{\left[ \left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}} \right]}^{9}}-{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\left[ \left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}} \right]}^{8}}1/3\text{x}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\left[ \left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}_{2}} \right]}^{3}}{{(1/3\text{x})}^{6}}-{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{7}}\left[ \left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}} \right] \right.$

$\left. 2{{(1/3\text{x})}^{7}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\ldots ...... \right\}$

$=\left( 1+\text{x}+2{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right){{\text{ }\!\!\{\!\!\text{ }\left( 3/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!]\!\!\text{ }}^{9}}-{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\left( {{3}^{7}}/{{2}^{8}} \right){{\text{x}}^{15}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots .+{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\left( 1\times 1/{{2}^{3}}\times {{3}^{3}} \right)-$ ${{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{7}}1/\left( {{2}^{2}}\times {{3}^{5}} \right)1/{{\text{x}}^{3}}+$….. }

Term independent of ' $\text{x}$ ' :

${{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}\times 1/\left( {{2}^{3}}\times {{3}^{3}} \right)-{{~}^{9}}{{\text{C}}_{7}}2/\left( {{2}^{2}}\times {{3}^{5}} \right)$

$=9!/\left( 6!\times 3! \right)\cdot 1/\left( 8\times 27 \right)-9!\left( 7!\times 2! \right)\cdot 1/\left( 2\times 243 \right)$

$=\left( 9.8.7.6! \right)/\left( 6!.3.2.1 \right)\times 1/\left( 8.27 \right)-\left( 9.8.7! \right)/\left( 7!.2 \right).1/\left( 2.243 \right)$

$=7/18-2/27=17/54$

(ii) ${{\left[ \left( 1/2 \right){{x}^{1/3}}+{{x}^{-1/5}} \right]}^{8}}$ 

Solution: 

General term ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\left[ \left( 1/2 \right){{\text{x}}^{1/3}} \right]}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}\cdot {{\left( {{\text{x}}^{-1/5}} \right)}^{\text{r}}}$

$={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}\cdot \left[ \frac{\text{n}-\text{r}}{{{\text{x}}^{3}}}{{\text{x}}^{-\text{r}/5}} \right]$

Here $\text{n}=8$

$~={{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(1/2)}^{8-\text{r}}}\times {{~}^{\left( 8-\text{r} \right)/3}}-\text{r}/5$

$40-8\text{r}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(1/2)}^{8-\text{r}}}\times 15$………………… (i)

Putting $\left( 40-8\text{r} \right)/15=0$, we have $\text{r}=5$

$\Rightarrow $ From (i), Term independent of ' $x$ ' :

${{\text{T}}_{6}}$ $~={{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}{{(1/2)}^{8-5}}$

$~=8!\left( 5!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3! \right)\cdot 1/{{2}^{3}}$ $~=8!\left( 5!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3! \right)\cdot 1/{{2}^{3}}$

$~=\left( 8.7.6\cdot !5 \right)/\left( 5!.3.2.1 \right)\cdot 1/8$

$~={{\text{T}}_{6}}=7$

Example 

Find the coefficient of ' x ' in the expansion of $\left( 1-2{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{5}} \right){{[1+\left( 1/x \right)]}^{8}}$ 

Solution: 

$\left( 1-2{{\text{x}}^{3}}+3{{\text{x}}^{5}} \right){{[1+\left( 1/\text{x} \right)]}^{8}}$

$=\left( 1-2{{\text{x}}^{3}}+3{{\text{x}}^{5}} \right)$ $\left[ 1+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\left( 1/\text{x} \right)+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{4}} \right)+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{5}} \right)+\ldots .+{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{8}}\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{8}} \right) \right.$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}$ $~=-2.{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+3{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}$

$~=-2.8!/\left( 2!\times 6! \right)+3.8!/\left( 4!\times 4! \right)$

$~=-2.\left( 8.7 \right)/2+3\left( 8.7.6.5 \right)/\left( 4.3.2.1 \right)$

$~=-56+210$

$~=154$

Example 

Prove that the ratio of the coefficient of ${{x}^{10}}$ in ${{\left( 1-{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{10}}$ and the term independent of ' x ' in ${{[x-\left( 2/x \right)]}^{10}}$ is 1 : 32. 

Solution: 

In ${{\left( 1-{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{10}}:{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{\text{r}}}$

Putting $\text{r}=5$

${{\text{T}}_{6}}=-{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}{{\text{x}}^{10}}$

$\Rightarrow $ Coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{10}}=-{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}$

In ${{[\text{x}-\left( 2/\text{x} \right)]}^{10}}:{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}=10{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{(\text{x})}^{10-\text{r}}}{{(2/\text{x})}^{\text{r}}}$

$={{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\cdot {{2}^{\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{10-2\text{r}}}$

Putting $10-2\text{r}=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{r}=5$

So term independent of $\text{x}:{{\text{T}}_{6}}={{(-1)}^{5}}{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}\cdot {{2}^{5}}$

Hence their ratio $=\left( -{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{5}} \right):\left( -32\cdot {{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{5}} \right)$

$=1:32$

Example 

If third term in the expansion of ${{\left( x+{{x}^{logx}} \right)}^{5}}$ is 10,00,000. Find the value of ' x '. 

Solution: 

Putting $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}=\text{z}$ in the given expression

We have : ${{\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}^{\text{z}}} \right)}^{5}}$

${{\text{T}}_{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }={{\text{T}}_{2+1}}={{~}^{5}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{(\text{x})}^{5-2}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{\text{z}}} \right)}^{2}}$

$={{~}^{5}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}}}$

$=\left[ 5!\left( 2!\times 3! \right) \right]{{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}+3}}$

$=\left( 5\times 4 \right)/2!{{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}+3}}$

$={{\text{T}}_{3}}=10{{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}+3}}$

If $\left( \text{logx} \right)=$ It means we are taking base 10

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }10,00,000=10\cdot {{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}+3}}$

Or ${{\text{x}}^{2\text{z}+3}}={{10}^{5}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\left( {{10}^{\text{z}}} \right)}^{2\text{z}+3}}={{10}^{5}}$

or ${{10}^{2z2+3z}}={{10}^{5}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2{{z}^{2}}+3z=5$

$\left[ \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x}=\text{z} \right]$

or $\left( \text{z}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{z}+5 \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ z}=1,-5/2$

or $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x}=1$ or $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x}=-5/2$

since $\text{x}=10$ or ${{10}^{-5/2}}$

Example 

If in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{m}}{{(1-x)}^{n}}$, the coefficients of ' x ' and ' x ', are ' 3 ' and ' -6 ' res. Find the value of ' m '. 

Solution: 

$\begin{matrix}
{{(1+\text{x})}^{\text{m}}}{{(1-\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}=\left[ {{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}+{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots .{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{m}}} \right] \\
\left[ {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}-\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}+\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots .+{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{n}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}} \right] \\
\end{matrix}$
Coefficient of $\text{x}=\text{m}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}-{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}\cdot {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}$
$\begin{array}{l} {} & ~=\left[ \text{m}!/\left( 1!\times \text{m}-1! \right) \right]\times 1-1\times \left[ \text{n}!/\left( 1!\times \text{n}-1! \right) \right] \\ \end{array}$

$~=\text{m}-\text{n}=3\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$

$~=-{{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\times {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}\times {{~}^{\text{m}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+\text{m}{{\text{C}}_{0}}\times \text{n}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$

$~=-\text{m}!/\left( 1!\times \text{m}-1! \right)\times \text{n}!/\left( 1!\times \text{n}-1! \right)+1\times \text{m}!/\left( 2!\times \text{m}-2! \right)$ $~+1\times \text{n}!/\left( 2!\times \text{n}-2! \right)$

$~=-\text{mn}+\text{m}\left( \text{m}-1 \right)/2+\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)/2=-6$

Or $~-2\text{mn}+\text{m}\left( \text{m}-1 \right)+\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)=-12$

Or $~-2\text{mn}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}-\text{m}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}-{{\text{n}}^{2}}-\text{n}=12$

Or $~{{(\text{m}-\text{n})}^{2}}-\left( \text{m}+\text{n} \right)=-12$

From (i), putting the value of $\left( m-n \right)$ :

$-9+\left( m+n \right)=12$

Or $\text{m}+\text{n}=21\ldots \ldots \ldots ...\left( \text{ii} \right)$

$\text{e}{{\text{g}}^{\text{n}}}\left( \text{i} \right)+\text{e}{{\text{g}}^{\text{n}}}\left( \text{ii} \right)=2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}=24$

m = 12

Example 

If the coefficients of $\left( 2r+1 \right)$ th term is ${{(r+2)}^{th~}}$ in the expansion of ${{(1+x)}^{43}}$ are equal, find ' r '. 

Solution: 

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ In }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(1+\text{x})}^{43}}\Rightarrow {{\text{T}}_{2\text{r}+1}}$ $~={{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{2\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{2\text{r}}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $~={{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{2\text{r}}}$

And ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+2}}$ $~={{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}+1}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}+1}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $~={{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}+1}}$

According to the questions:

${{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{2\text{r}}}$ $~={{~}^{43}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}+1}}$

2r + r +1 = 43

Or 3r = 42

r = 14

Example 

If the coefficients of ' 4 'th and ' 13 ' th terms in the expansion of ${{\left[ {{x}^{2}}+\left( 1/x \right) \right]}^{n}}$ be equal, then find the term which independent of ' x '. 

Solution: 

${{\text{T}}_{4}}={{\text{T}}_{3+1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{\text{n}-3}}\cdot 1/{{\text{x}}^{3}}$

Coefficient $~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{13}}={{\text{T}}_{12+1}}$ $~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{12}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{\text{n}-12}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1/{{\text{x}}^{12}}$

Coefficient $~=\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{12}}$ $~=\text{n}{{\text{C}}_{12}}$

According to the question:

${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$ $~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{12}}$

$\Rightarrow $ n = 12 + 3

n = 15

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Expansion }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ $~={{\left[ {{\text{x}}^{2}}+\left( 1/\text{x} \right) \right]}^{15}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}$ $~=15{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\cdot {{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{15-\text{r}}}\cdot 1/{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}$ $~={{~}^{15}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{30-3\text{r}}}\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots $(i)

Putting

30 – 3r = 0

$\Rightarrow $ r = 10

From (i) ${{\text{T}}_{11}}={{~}^{15}}{{\text{C}}_{10}}=15!/\left( 10!\times 5! \right)=\left( 15\times 14\times 13\times 12\times 11 \right)/\left( 5\times 4\times 3\times 2\times \right)$

$=3003.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Example 

In the expansion of ${{(a-b)}^{n}},n\geq5$, if the sum of the 5 th and 6 th terms is zero. Find (a/b) in terms of ' n '. 

Solution: 

$\mathrm{T}_5=\mathrm{T}_{4+1}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_4(\mathrm{a})^{\mathrm{n}-4}(-\mathrm{b})^4$

$\mathrm{~T}_5={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-4} \mathrm{~b}^4$

$\mathrm{~T}_6=\mathrm{T}_{5+1}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_5(\mathrm{a})^{\mathrm{n}-5}(-\mathrm{b})^5=-{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-5} \mathrm{~b}^5$

$\mathrm{~T}_5+\mathrm{T}_6=0$

$\Rightarrow{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-4} \mathrm{~b}^4-{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-5} \mathrm{~b}^5=0$

$\text { or }{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-4} \mathrm{~b}^4=\mathrm{nC}_5 \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-5} \mathrm{~b}^5$

$\text { or } \Rightarrow[\mathrm{n} ! / 4 ! \times(\mathrm{n}-4) !] \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-4}$

$\text { or }=[\mathrm{n} ! / 5 ! \times(\mathrm{n}-5)] \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-5} \mathrm{~b}$

$\text { or } \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-4} /[(\mathrm{n}-4)(\mathrm{n}-5)]=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-5} \mathrm{~b} / 5(\mathrm{n}-5)$

$\text { or } \mathrm{a}(\mathrm{n}-4) /-\mathrm{a}-5=\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{n}-4) / 5$

$\text { or } \mathrm{a}[\mathrm{n}-4)-(\mathrm{n}-5)]=\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{n}-4) / 5$

$\text { or } \mathrm{a}=[(\mathrm{n}-4) / 5] \cdot \mathrm{b}$

$\text { or } \mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}=(\mathrm{n}-4) / 5$

Example 

Find the coefficient of ${{x}^{r}}$ in the expansion of ${{[x+\left( 1/x \right)]}^{n}}$, if it occurs. 

Solution: 

General term: ${{\text{T}}_{\text{p}+1}}$ $~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{{(\text{x})}^{\text{n}-\text{p}}}{{(1/\text{x})}^{\text{p}}}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{p}+1}}$ $~={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2\text{p}}}\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots $(i)

Putting $\text{n}-2\text{p}=\text{r}$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{p}=\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/2$

From (i) ${{\text{T}}_{\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/2+1}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}$

Coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/2}}$

Example 

Prove that the coefficient of the term independent of ' y ' in the expansion of ${{\left[ \left( y+1 \right)/\left( {{y}^{2/3}}-{{y}^{1/3}}+1 \right)-\left( y-1 \right)/\left( y-{{y}^{1/2}} \right) \right]}^{10}}$ is 210. 

Solution: 

We have $\left( y+1 \right)/\left( {{y}^{2/3}}-{{y}^{1/3}}+1 \right)$

Putting $\text{y}={{\text{t}}^{3}}$, we have

$\begin{array}{l} =\left( {{\text{t}}^{3}}+{{1}^{3}} \right)/\left( {{\text{t}}^{2}}-\text{t}+1 \right)=\left( \text{t}+1 \right)\left( {{\text{t}}^{2}}-\text{t}+1 \right)/\left( {{\text{t}}^{2}}-\text{t}+1 \right) \\  =\text{t}+1 \\ \end{array}$

$\left( \text{y}+1 \right)/\left( {{\text{y}}^{2/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{1/3}}+1 \right)={{\text{y}}^{1/3}}+1$

and putting $\text{y}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$ in $\left( \text{y}-1 \right)/\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}^{1/2}} \right)$ :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}-1 \right)/\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}-\text{a} \right)=\left( \text{a}+1 \right)\left( \text{a}-1 \right)/\left[ \text{a}\left( \text{a}-1 \right) \right] \\  =\left( \text{a}+1 \right)/\text{a}=1+1/\text{a} \\ \Rightarrow  \left( \text{y}-1 \right)/\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}^{1/2}} \right)=1+1/\sqrt{4} \\ \Rightarrow  {{\left[ \left( \text{y}+1 \right)/\left( {{\text{y}}^{2/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{1/3}}+1 \right)-\left( \text{y}-1 \right)/\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}^{1/2}} \right) \right]}^{10}} \\  ={{\left( {{\text{y}}^{1/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{-1/2}} \right)}^{10}} \\ \end{array}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}-1 \right)/\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}-\text{a} \right)=\left( \text{a}+1 \right)\left( \text{a}-1 \right)/\left[ \text{a}\left( \text{a}-1 \right) \right] \\
{} & =\left( \text{a}+1 \right)/\text{a}=1+1/\text{a} \\
\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \left( \text{y}-1 \right)/\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}^{1/2}} \right)=1+1/\sqrt{4} \\
\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{\left[ \left( \text{y}+1 \right)/\left( {{\text{y}}^{2/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{1/3}}+1 \right)-\left( \text{y}-1 \right)/\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}^{1/2}} \right) \right]}^{10}} \\
{} & ={{\left( {{\text{y}}^{1/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{-1/2}} \right)}^{10}} \\
\end{array}$

In ${{\left( {{\text{y}}^{1/3}}-{{\text{y}}^{-1/2}} \right)}^{10}}$,

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\left( {{\text{y}}^{1/3}} \right)}^{10-\text{r}}}{{\left( -{{\text{y}}^{-1/2}} \right)}^{\text{r}}}$

$={{(-1)}^{\text{r}}}{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( \text{y} \right)\left[ \frac{10-\text{r}}{3}-\frac{\text{r}}{2} \right]$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{(-1)}^{\text{r}10}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{y}}^{\left( 20-5\text{r} \right)/6}}$

Putting $\left( 20-5\text{r} \right)/6=0$

or $r=4$

Putting this value in (1) ${{\text{T}}_{5}}={{(-1)}^{4}}{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}$

$~=10!/\left( 6!\times 4! \right)=\left( 10\times 9\times 8\times 7 \right)/\left( 4\times 3\times 2\times 1 \right)$

${{\text{T}}_{5}}$= 210

Example 

${{x}^{4r}}$ occurs in the expansion of ${{\left[ x+\left( 1/{{x}^{2}} \right) \right]}^{4n}}$, prove that its coefficients is : 

$\left. =4n!/\left[ 4\left( n-r \right)/3 \right)!\times \frac{4}{3}\left( 2n+r \right)! \right]$ 

Solution: 

In ${{\left[ \text{x}+\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right) \right]}^{4\text{n}}},{{\text{T}}_{\text{p}+1}}={{~}^{4\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{{(\text{x})}^{4\text{n}-\text{p}}}{{\left( \frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right)}^{\text{p}}}$

${{\text{T}}_{\text{p}+1}}={{~}^{4\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{p}}}{{\text{x}}^{4\text{n}-3\text{p}}}$………… (i)

Putting : $4\text{n}-3\text{p}=4\text{r}$

Or $4\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/3=\text{p}$

From (i) ${{\text{T}}_{\text{p}+1}}$ $~={{~}^{4n}}{{\text{C}}_{4\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)}}{{\text{x}}^{4\text{r}}}$

Coefficient of ${{\text{x}}^{4\text{r}}}$ $~={{~}^{4n}}{{\text{C}}_{4\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)/3}}$

$~=4\text{n}!/\left[ \left( \frac{4\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)}{3} \right)!\times \frac{4}{3}\left( 2\text{n}+\text{r} \right)! \right]$

Example 

Find the coefficient of ${{x}^{50}}$ in ${{(1+x)}^{41}}{{\left( 1-x+{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{40}}$ 

Solution: 

${{(1+\text{x})}^{41}}{{\left( 1-\text{x}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{40}}$ $=\left( 1+\text{x} \right){{(1+\text{x})}^{40}}{{\left( 1-\text{x}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{40}}$

$=\left( 1+\text{x} \right){{\left[ \left( 1+\text{x} \right)\left( 1-\text{x}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right) \right]}^{40}}$

$=\left( 1+\text{x} \right){{\left( 1+{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)}^{40}}$

$\text{ General }\text{ Term }$ $={{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}=\left( 1+\text{x} \right)\left[ {{}^{40}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)}^{\text{r}}} \right]$

$={{}^{40}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( 1+\text{x} \right){{\text{x}}^{3\text{r}}}$

$={{}^{40}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{3\text{r}}}+{{\text{x}}^{3\text{r}+1}} \right)$

Here either 3r = 50 or 3r + 1 = 50

$\Rightarrow $ r $=\left( 50/3 \right)\text{ or }\left( 49/3 \right)$

The value of ' $r$ ' is a fraction, so it doesn't contain the term ${{x}^{50}}$. So coefficient of ${{x}^{50}}$ is ' 0 '.

Example 

Show that the term independent of ' x ' in the expansion of 

${{[x+\left( 1/x \right)]}^{2n}}~is~\left[ 1.3.5....\left( 2n-1 \right)/\left( n! \right) \right]{{2}^{n}}$ 

Solution: 

General Term ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{}^{2\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{(\text{x})}^{2\text{n}-\text{r}}}{{(1/\text{x})}^{\text{r}}}$

$={{}^{2n}}{{C}_{r}}\cdot {{x}^{2n-2r}}$…………. (i)

Here 2n – 2r = 0

Or n = r

From (i) ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{}^{2\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}$

$=2\text{n}!/\left( \text{n}!\times \text{n}! \right)$

$=\left[ 2\text{n}\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\ldots \ldots \ldots ..3.2.1 \right]/\left( \text{n}!\times \text{n}! \right)$

$=\left\{ 2\text{n}\left( 2\text{n}-2 \right)\ldots \ldots \ldots ...4.2 \right\}\{\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{n}-3 \right)$…… 3.1.} / $\left( \text{n}!\times \text{n}! \right)$

$=\left[ {{2}^{\text{n}}}\left\{ \text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\ldots \ldots ..2.1. \right\} \right]\{\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)$………. 5.3.1} / $\left( \text{n}!\times \text{n}! \right)$

$={{2}^{\text{n}}}\cdot \text{n}!\left\{ \left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\ldots \ldots .5.3.1. \right\}/\left( \text{n}!\times \text{n}! \right)$

$=\{1.3.5.$…$..\left( 2\text{n}-1 \right)\}{{2}^{\text{n}}}/\text{n }\text{ ! }$

Example 

The ${{3}^{rd~}},{{4}^{th~}}$ and ${{5}^{th~}}$ terms in the expansion of ${{(x+a)}^{n}}$ are respectively ' 84 ', '280' and ' 560 ', find the value of ' x ', ' a ' and ' n '. 

Solution: 

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}\cdot {{\text{a}}^{\text{r}}}$

Putting $\text{r}=2,3$ and 4 respectively

${{\text{T}}_{3}} ={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}\cdot {{\text{a}}^{2}}=84......(i)$

${{\text{T}}_{4}} ={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-3}}{{\text{a}}^{3}}=280...........(ii)$

And ${{\text{T}}_{5}} ={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-4}}{{\text{a}}^{4}}=560................(iii)$

egn (i) egn (iii) : $\left[ {{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-2}}{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right]\left[ {{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-4}}{{\text{a}}^{4}} \right]=84\times 560$

$=\left\{ \text{n}!/\left[ 2!\times \left( \text{n}-2 \right)! \right] \right\}\times \left\{ \text{n}!/\left[ 4!\times \left( \text{n}-4 \right)! \right] \right\}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{2\text{n}-6}}{{\text{a}}^{6}}=84\times 560$

or $\left[ \text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)/2\left] \times \right[\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\left( \text{n}-3 \right)/4! \right]{{\text{x}}^{2\text{n}-6}}{{\text{a}}^{6}}=84\times 560$……. (iv)

Squaring of $\text{e}{{\text{g}}^{\text{n}}}$ (ii), we have :

${{\left( {{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}-3}}{{\text{a}}^{3}} \right)}^{2}}={{280}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow  {{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}\times {{\text{x}}^{2\text{n}-6}}\times {{\text{a}}^{6}}={{280}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow  =\left\{ \text{n}!/\left[ 3!\times \left( \text{n}-3 \right)! \right] \right\}\times \text{n}!/\left[ 3!\times \left( \text{n}-3! \right]\times {{\text{x}}^{2\text{n}-6}}{{\text{a}}^{6}}={{280}^{2}} \right.$

or $\left[ \text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)/6\left] \times \right[\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)\left( \text{n}-2 \right)\left( \text{n}-3 \right)/3! \right]\text{x}{{\text{x}}^{2\text{n}-6}}{{\text{a}}^{6}}=280\times 280$………… (v)

$\text{ egn }\left( \text{v} \right)\div \text{ egn }\text{ (iv) }$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ or } & 4\left( \text{n}-2 \right)/3\left( \text{n}-3 \right)=5/3 \\ \text{ or } & 4\text{n}-8=5\text{n}-15 \\ {} & \text{n}=7 \\ \end{array}$

Putting this value in (i), (ii) and (iii):

${{}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{5}}{{\text{a}}^{2}}=84\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots ...(\text{ vi) }$

${{}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}^{4}}{{\text{a}}^{3}}=280\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots ...(\text{ vii) }$

${{}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{\text{a}}^{4}}=560\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots ...(\text{ viii }\text{ )}$

$\text{e}{{\text{g}}^{\text{n}}}\text{ (vii) }\div \text{e}{{\text{g}}^{\text{n}}}\text{ (vi) }\text{ : }$

$\left( {{}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}^{4}}{{\text{a}}^{3}} \right)/\left( {{}^{7}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{5}}{{\text{a}}^{2}} \right)=280/84$

a. $\left[ 7!/\left( 3!\times 4! \right)\left] / \right[7!/\left( 2!\times 5! \right) \right]\times =10/3$

or $\frac{2!5!}{3!4!}\frac{\text{a}}{\text{x}} =\frac{10}{3}$

or $\left( 5/3 \right)\times \text{a}/\text{x}=10/3$

or $\text{a}=2\text{x}$

Putting this value in egn (vi):

$\begin{matrix} {{}^{7}}{{C}_{2}}\cdot {{x}^{5}}\cdot 4{{x}^{2}}=84 \\ \text{ or }\frac{7!}{2!5!}{{x}^{7}}=21 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{42}{2}{{x}^{7}}=21 \\ {{x}^{7}}=1 \\ \Rightarrow x=1 \\ \end{matrix}$

Putting this value in (ix) $=\text{a}=2$

Example 

Let ' n ' be a positive integer. If the coefficients of second, third and fourth terms in ${{(1+x)}^{2}}$ are in arithmetic progression, then find the value of ' n '. 

Solution: 

General Term : ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}+1}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{r}}}$

$\Rightarrow {{2}^{\text{nd }}}$ Term : ${{\text{T}}_{2}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{x}$

Coefficient $={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}$

$\Rightarrow {{3}^{\text{rd }}}$ Term : ${{\text{T}}_{3}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$

Coefficient $={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$

Similarly coefficient of ${{4}^{\text{th }}}$ term $={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$

These are in A.P., so.

$2{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_2={ }^{\mathrm{n}} C_1+{ }^{\mathrm{n}} C_3$

$\Rightarrow 2[\mathrm{n} ! /\{2 ! \times(\mathrm{n}-2) !\}]=\mathrm{n} ! /\{1 ! \times(\mathrm{n}-1) !\}+\mathrm{n} ! /\{3 ! \times(\mathrm{n}-3) !\}$

$\text{or} \mathrm{n} ! /(\mathrm{n}-2) !=\mathrm{n} ![1 /(\mathrm{n}-1) !+1 / 6(\mathrm{n}-3) !]$

$\text{or} 1 /[(\mathrm{n}-2) \times(\mathrm{n}-3) !=1 /[\mathrm{n}-1) \times(\mathrm{n}-2) \times(\mathrm{n}-3) !]+1 / 6(\mathrm{n}-3) !$

$\text{or} 1 /(\mathrm{n}-2)-1 /[(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)]=1 / 6$

$\text{or} (\mathrm{n}-1-1) /[(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)]=1 / 6$

$\text{or} (\mathrm{n}-2) /[(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)]=1 / 6$

$\text{or} \mathrm{n}-1=6$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7$

Example 

(a) The ${{6}^{th~}}$ term in the expansion of ${{\left[ \left( 1/{{x}^{8/3}} \right)+{{x}^{2}}lo{{g}_{10}} \right]}^{8}}$ is 5600. Prove that x = 10. 

Solution: 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{T}}_{6}}={{\text{T}}_{5+1}}={{}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}{{\left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{8/3}} \right)}^{8-5}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}{{}^{\text{x}}} \right)}^{5}} \\ \text{ or }{{}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{5}}\times \left( 1/{{\text{x}}^{8}} \right)\times {{\text{x}}^{10}}{{\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)}^{5}}=5600 \\ \Rightarrow \left[ 8!/\left( 5!\times 3! \right) \right]\times {{\text{x}}^{2}}\times {{\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}} \right)}^{5}}=5600 \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \left[ 8.7.6./6 \right]\times {{\text{x}}^{2}}\times {{\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\text{x} \right)}^{5}}=5600$

$\text{ or }{{\text{x}}^{2}}{{\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}} \right)}^{\text{x}}}=100={{10}^{2}}$

Clearly $\text{x}=10$ satisfied as $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}10=1$

if $x>10$ or $<10$, the result will change in inequality.

(b) If ${{(\sqrt{P}+Q)}^{n}}=1+f$ where 1 and n are positive integers, n being odd, and \[\mathrm{0}\le \mathrm{f1}\], then show that $\left( l+f \right)f={{k}^{n}}$ where $P-{{Q}^{2}}=k>0$ and $\sqrt{\mathbf{P}}-\mathbf{Q}<1$ < 1 . 

Proof: 

$\text{ Given }\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q}<1$

$\left( \sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q} \right)\left( \sqrt{\text{P}}+\text{Q} \right)>0\text{ (given) }$

$\therefore $ $\left( \sqrt{\text{P}}+\text{Q} \right)>0\left[ \text{ l }\text{ and }\text{ t }\text{ both }\text{ are }\left( +\text{ve} \right)\text{ no}\text{. } \right]$

$\therefore 0<{{(\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}<1$

Now let ${{(\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}=\text{{f}'}$

$\therefore \text{ l}+\text{f}-\text{{f}'}={{(\sqrt{\text{P}}+\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}-{{(\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}$

$\therefore $ RHS contains even powers of $\sqrt{\text{P}}(\therefore \text{n}$ is odd)

$\Rightarrow $ RHS is an integer

Since RHS and I are integers

$\therefore \text{f}-\text{{f}'}$ is also integer

$\therefore \Rightarrow \text{ f}-\text{{f}'}=0\therefore 1<\text{f}-\text{{f}'}<1$

or $\text{f}=\text{{f}'}$

$\therefore \left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\text{f}=\left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\text{{f}'}={{(\sqrt{\text{P}}+\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}-{{(\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}={{\left( \text{P}-{{\text{Q}}^{2}} \right)}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{k}}^{\text{n}}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\text{f}=\left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\text{{f}'}={{(\sqrt{\text{P}}+\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}-{{(\sqrt{\text{P}}-\text{Q})}^{\text{n}}}={{\left( \text{P}-{{\text{Q}}^{2}} \right)}^{\text{n}}}={{\text{k}}^{\text{n}}}$

Example 

If ${{(2+\sqrt{3})}^{n}}=I+f$ where $I$ and $\text{n}$ are positive integers and 0 < f < 1, show that (i) I is an odd integer and (ii) (1 + f) (1 – f) = 1. 

Solution: 

(i) Now $0<2-\sqrt{3}<1$, since $2-\sqrt{3}=0.268$ (approx.)

$\therefore 0<{{(2-\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}<1$; we can take ${{(2-\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}$ as $\text{{f}'}$.

Now ${{(2+\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}+{{(2-\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}=\text{l}+\text{f}+\text{{f}'}$

But L.H.S. $=2\left\{ {{2}^{\text{n}}}+{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{}^{\text{n}-2}}{{(\sqrt{3})}^{2}}+{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{2}^{\text{n}-4}}{{(\sqrt{3})}^{4}}+\ldots \right\}=$ an integer (in fact an even integer)

$\therefore \text{ RHS}=\text{I}+\text{f}+\text{{f}'}=$ an even integer

Also $\text{f}+\text{{f}'}=1$, since $\text{f}$ and $\text{{f}'}$ are both positive proper fractions.

$\therefore \text{ I}=$ an even integer $-1=$ an odd integer.

(ii) $\left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\left( \text{l}-\text{f} \right)=\left( \text{l}+\text{f} \right)\left( {\text{{f}'}} \right)={{(2+\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}\cdot {{(2-\sqrt{3})}^{\text{n}}}$

$={{(4-3)}^{\text{n}}}={{1}^{\text{n}}}=1$

$={{(4-3)}^{\text{n}}}={{1}^{\text{n}}}=1$

Example 

Let $R={{(5\sqrt{5}+11)}^{2n+1}}$ and $f=R-\left[ R \right]$, where [] denotes the greatest integer function. Prove that $Rf={{4}^{2n+1}}$. 

Solution: 

Greatest integer function is defined as follows:

$\left[ \text{x} \right]=$ greatest integer $\le \text{x}$

In the case of positive number, $x$

$\left[ \text{x} \right]=$ integral part of $\text{x}$

$\therefore f=R-\left[ R \right]$ implies that $f$ is the fractional part of $R$.

$\therefore 0<\text{f}<1$

Since $144>125>121,\sqrt{125}=5\sqrt{5}$ lies between 11 and 12 .

$\therefore 0<5\sqrt{5}-11<1$ and hence ${{(5\sqrt{5}-11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}$ will also be a proper fraction.

Let $g={{(5\sqrt{5}-11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}$

Now $\left[ R \right]+f-g=R-g$

$={{(5\sqrt{5}-11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}-{{(5\sqrt{5}+11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}-{{(5\sqrt{5}-11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}$

$=2\left\{ \left( 2\text{n}+1 \right){{\text{C}}_{1}}{{(5\sqrt{5})}^{2\text{n}}}\cdot {{11}^{1}}+{{}^{\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{(5\sqrt{5})}^{2\text{n}-2}}\cdot {{11}^{2}}+\ldots \right\}$

$=\text{ an }\text{ even }\text{ integer}\text{. }$

Since $\left[ R \right]$ is an integer, the above implies $f-g=0$ (i.e.) $f=g$

Hence $\text{Rf}$ $=\text{Rg}={{(5\sqrt{5}+11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}\cdot {{(5\sqrt{5}-11)}^{2\text{n}+1}}$

$={{(125-121)}^{2\text{n}+1}}={{4}^{2\text{n}+1}}$

$~={{(125-121)}^{2\text{n}+1}}={{4}^{2\text{n}+1}}$

examples.title: If $(1+x)^n=C_0+C_1 x+C_2 x^2+\ldots .+C_n x^n$ then $C_0 C_2+C_1 C_3+C_2 C_4+C_{n-2} C_n$ equals
The coefficient of the term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x+2 x^3\right)\left(\frac{3}{2} x^2-\frac{1}{3 x}\right)^9$ is
